title: Felting over a resist
subtitle: The core method for making seamless 3D felt forms: hats, bags, vessels, and slippers
excerpt: A resist is a waterproof template placed inside or under roving before felting. The wool felts around it but not to it, so the resist can be removed to reveal a hollow 3D form. This tutorial teaches the resist principle using a flat template to make an open pouch.
sourceNotes: The resist method is fundamental to three-dimensional wet felting. The flat-template resist producing a flat-bottomed hollow form is the most accessible variant of the principle. The technique is observation-led with no single PD text source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A waterproof template or form placed inside wet-felting roving to shape the finished piece. The wool felts around the resist but not to its slick surface, so the resist slides out to reveal a hollow interior.
A technique that uses hot water, soap, and agitation to cause wool fibre scales to open, migrate, and permanently lock together into a non-woven fabric.

content.text: is a waterproof template, usually cut from thick plastic, foam rubber, or polystyrene. When roving is layered over both faces of the resist and

, the wool felts to itself but not to the slick resist surface, and the resist slides out to leave a hollow form. This tutorial uses a flat oval resist (cut from a plastic folder) to produce a small open pouch about 16 x 12 cm after felting.

items.name: Flat resist, cut from a plastic folder or bubble wrap: oval, 22 x 16 cm
Sharp scissors (to cut the opening after felting)

content.text: Step 1: Lay roving on one face of the resist

Lay the resist flat on the bamboo mat. Cover the top face with three layers of roving in cross-hatched directions, wrapping the excess 3-4 cm over the edge of the resist onto the underside. The turn-over at the edge is what seals the sides of the finished pouch. Do not fold the roving under the resist: just wrap it over the edge and press the wrapped portion flat.
Step 2: Flip and lay the other face

Wet down and lightly felt the first face before flipping. Carefully flip the whole layout over so the unfelt side faces up. The already-damp roving on the bottom face holds the layout together enough for the flip. Now lay three more cross-hatched layers of roving on this face, again wrapping 3-4 cm over the edges.
Step 3: Felt both faces and the sides

Wet down, soap, and rub both faces in turn, flipping every few minutes. Rub the folded edge firmly with thumbs and fingers to encourage the two faces to join around the resist perimeter. After 20-30 minutes, test the felt with the pinch test: both faces should resist the pinch, and the edges should feel joined rather than peeling apart.
Step 4: Remove the resist and cut the opening

Squeeze the felted form from outside the resist to slide the resist toward one edge. Cut a small slit in the felt at that edge and slide the resist out. Trim the cut to the opening size and shape you want. Re-wet the opening edge with soapy water and rub it firmly between finger and thumb to bind any raw edge fibres.

Continue rubbing the empty pouch to full it: the two walls should bind further and the overall form should shrink and tighten. Roll in the bamboo mat for 2-3 minutes in each orientation. Rinse, shape while wet, and dry flat.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems specific to flat-resist felting.

items.symptom: The two faces of the pouch have not joined at the edges: they peel apart when the resist is removed
items.cause: The edge-wrap was too narrow or the edges were not rubbed firmly enough during felting.
items.fix: Before removing the resist, rub the edges firmly between fingers with fresh hot soapy water. If the faces still won't join after 10 minutes of edge rubbing, the felt is not ready. Continue felting the faces before trying the edges again.

items.symptom: Resist is very difficult to remove
items.cause: Roving has wrapped fully around the resist rather than staying on the face only, trapping the resist inside.
items.fix: Cut a wider slit in the felt at the edge. If the resist is fully encased, cut open one full side of the pouch to extract it, then re-close the opening by felting or sewing.
